content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 1, 1992


Gordon C. Phillips
City Attorney
415 Diamond Street
Redondo Beach, CA 90277-0270

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-92-419

Dear Mr. Phillips:

This letter is in response to your request for
reconsideration of the advice provided to Gloria Valenti, city
clerk for the City of Redondo Beach, regarding the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 (Valenti
Advice Letter, No. I-91-264.)

QUESTION

Under the provisions of the Act, may the word "Mayor" be
included in notices in which reference is made to a hearing
"before the Mayor and City Council"?

CONCLUSION

Under the provisions of the Act, the word "Mayor" may be
included in notices in which reference is made to a hearing before
the mayor and city council if the notice is required by law and
use of the word "mayor" is necessary to the mailing.

FACTS

The legislative body of the City of Redondo Beach is
comprised of a 5-person city council. The councilmembers are
elected by district. The mayor is elected at large to represent
the city. The mayor may veto any action of the city council or

_______________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-419
Page 2

vote to break a tie. The Legislative Counsel has advised the city
that the mayor is to be considered in determining whether or not a
quorum is present for purposes of the Brown Act.

The city has traditionally recognized the mayor as a
participant in hearings. For example, it has been the practice,
in the adoption of resolutions, to state: "The Mayor and City
Council Do Hereby Resolve...."

Since the mayor is not part of the legislative body, and
because the mayor may veto any action or vote to break a tie, the
mayor is a person before whom a hearing is held. It would be
inappropriate, in view of the city charter, to include the mayor
as part of the city council. Thus, it is your belief that the
word mayor should be included in notices of hearings before the
mayor and the city council.

ANALYSIS

As we have previously advised the City of Redondo Beach
(Valenti, supra,) the Act prohibits mass mailings at public
expense. (Section 89001.) "Mass mailing" means over two hundred
substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include mail
which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter, or
other inquiry. (Section 82041.5; Regulation 18901(a)(4).)

A "mass mailing" is prohibited if it features an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, or includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing if the mailing is prepared or sent
in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the
elected officer. (Regulation 18901(a).) Accordingly, unless an
exception applies, the word "mayor" may not be included in notices
of city council hearings.

An exception which may be applicable to your facts is that of
Regulation 18901(b)(7) which provides that a mass mailing of a
legal notice sent as required by law, and in which use of the
elected officer's name, office, title, or signature is necessary
to the mailing, is not prohibited by Section 89001. Thus, if
notices of hearings before the city council are required by law,
and if the law requires inclusion of the term "mayor and city
council" whenever the mayor is not a member of the city council
but acts as the presiding officer, then we would conclude that
inclusion of the term "mayor" in such notices would be "necessary"
to the mailing and would not violate the mass mailing provisions
of the Act.

In addition, if you conclude that in order to accomplish the
legal purpose of a notice the term "mayor" must be included in the
notice, we would agree that such inclusion does not violate the

Our File No. I-92-419
Page 3

provisions of Regulation 18901.² However, at the present time, we
cannot conceive of any such requirement.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.³

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:aa


2
In rendering advice, the Commission is not a finder of fact.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

3
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
